sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
A clinical research team studied glucose homeostasis by performing oral glucose tolerance tests (OGTT) on three groups of subjects: 20 healthy controls (Group A), 20 individuals with impaired glucose tolerance (IGT, Group B), and 20 patients with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM, Group C). After an overnight fast, each subject ingested 75 g of glucose dissolved in 300 mL of water. Blood samples were drawn at 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 minutes for measurement of plasma glucose and serum insulin concentrations.

Table 1. OGTT Results (mean ± SD)
| Time (min) | Group A Glucose (mg/dL) | Group A Insulin (μU/mL) | Group B Glucose (mg/dL) | Group B Insulin (μU/mL) | Group C Glucose (mg/dL) | Group C Insulin (μU/mL) |
|------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 0 | 88 ± 6 | 8.2 ± 2.1 | 102 ± 8 | 14.5 ± 4.2 | 138 ± 15 | 18.1 ± 6.8 |
| 30 | 142 ± 12 | 62.4 ± 15.3 | 168 ± 18 | 48.2 ± 12.1 | 215 ± 22 | 32.6 ± 10.4 |
| 60 | 128 ± 10 | 71.8 ± 18.2 | 182 ± 20 | 72.4 ± 18.8 | 268 ± 28 | 58.4 ± 15.2 |
| 90 | 108 ± 8 | 45.2 ± 12.4 | 162 ± 16 | 68.1 ± 16.4 | 252 ± 25 | 62.8 ± 18.1 |
| 120 | 92 ± 7 | 18.4 ± 5.8 | 148 ± 14 | 52.3 ± 14.2 | 228 ± 24 | 55.2 ± 16.8 |

The researchers also measured HbA1c levels: Group A = 5.1%, Group B = 6.0%, Group C = 7.8%. They noted that Group B subjects exhibited higher fasting insulin than controls despite only moderately elevated fasting glucose, suggesting early insulin resistance with compensatory hyperinsulinemia.

The incretin hormones GLP-1 and GIP, released from intestinal L-cells and K-cells respectively in response to oral nutrients, were also measured. Group A showed robust GLP-1 secretion peaking at 30 minutes, while Groups B and C had attenuated GLP-1 responses (approximately 60% and 40% of control levels, respectively).
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Oral glucose tolerance test curves showing plasma glucose (solid lines, left axis) and serum insulin (dashed lines, right axis) over 120 minutes for Group A (normal), Group B (IGT), and Group C (T2DM).
questions.stem: Group B subjects have higher fasting insulin (14.5 μU/mL) than controls (8.2 μU/mL) but only moderately elevated fasting glucose (102 vs. 88 mg/dL). Which pathophysiological concept best explains this pattern?

choices.A: Type 1 diabetes with autoimmune destruction of beta cells
choices.B: Compensatory hyperinsulinemia in response to peripheral insulin resistance
choices.C: Primary hyperaldosteronism causing fluid retention and dilution of glucose
choices.D: Insulinoma causing excessive unregulated insulin secretion

explanations.A: Type 1 diabetes involves autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells, leading to absolute insulin deficiency with very low or absent insulin levels. Group B subjects have elevated insulin levels (14.5 μU/mL, nearly double controls), which is the opposite of what Type 1 diabetes produces. Additionally, Type 1 diabetes typically presents with severe hyperglycemia, not the moderate elevation seen here.
explanations.B: Correct. In early insulin resistance, target tissues (skeletal muscle, adipose, liver) exhibit reduced responsiveness to insulin's signaling cascade — typically due to defects in insulin receptor substrate phosphorylation, PI3K/Akt pathway activation, and GLUT4 translocation. To maintain near-normal fasting glucose, pancreatic β-cells compensate by secreting more insulin. This compensatory hyperinsulinemia is the hallmark of the pre-diabetic state and explains why Group B maintains fasting glucose at only 102 mg/dL despite impaired insulin action. As resistance progresses and β-cell compensation fails (Group C), both fasting glucose and the glucose-insulin mismatch worsen.
explanations.C: Primary hyperaldosteronism causes sodium retention, hypertension, and hypokalemia but does not directly affect glucose metabolism or insulin secretion in the pattern observed. The glucose-insulin relationship in Group B specifically reflects a disorder of insulin sensitivity, not fluid balance or adrenal pathology.
explanations.D: An insulinoma would cause inappropriate insulin secretion leading to hypoglycemia, not the mild hyperglycemia observed in Group B. Insulinoma patients typically present with Whipple's triad: symptoms of hypoglycemia, documented low blood glucose, and symptom relief with glucose administration. Group B's fasting glucose of 102 mg/dL rules out insulinoma.

questions.stem: The attenuated GLP-1 response in Groups B and C has what direct consequence for glucose homeostasis?

choices.A: Reduced incretin-mediated potentiation of glucose-stimulated insulin secretion from β-cells
choices.B: Increased glucagon secretion from α-cells due to loss of GLP-1's inhibitory effect
choices.C: Decreased intestinal glucose absorption, paradoxically worsening hyperglycemia
choices.D: Both A and B contribute to impaired glucose control

explanations.A: This is partially correct. GLP-1 binds to receptors on pancreatic β-cells and potentiates glucose-stimulated insulin secretion (the incretin effect accounts for 50–70% of postprandial insulin release). Reduced GLP-1 would indeed diminish this amplification. However, GLP-1 also has important effects on α-cells, making this answer incomplete.
explanations.B: This is also partially correct. GLP-1 normally suppresses glucagon secretion from pancreatic α-cells. Reduced GLP-1 activity disinhibits α-cells, leading to inappropriate glucagon release that stimulates hepatic glucose output (glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis), worsening postprandial hyperglycemia. However, this is only one of GLP-1's effects.
explanations.C: GLP-1 does not enhance intestinal glucose absorption; glucose absorption occurs via SGLT1 and GLUT2 transporters independent of incretin signaling. In fact, GLP-1 slows gastric emptying, which would slow glucose delivery to the intestine. Reduced GLP-1 would slightly accelerate gastric emptying, increasing the rate of glucose absorption and worsening postprandial glucose spikes, but this is not the primary mechanism of impaired homeostasis.
explanations.D: Correct. GLP-1 has dual actions critical for glucose homeostasis: it potentiates glucose-stimulated insulin secretion from β-cells (incretin effect) and suppresses glucagon release from α-cells. When GLP-1 is attenuated, both arms are impaired — insulin secretion is inadequately amplified after meals, and glucagon remains inappropriately elevated, stimulating hepatic glucose production. This dual defect explains why GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g., semaglutide, liraglutide) are effective T2DM treatments: they restore both the insulinotropic and glucagonostatic effects. Additionally, GLP-1 slows gastric emptying and promotes satiety, and reduced levels may contribute to postprandial glucose spikes.

questions.stem: HbA1c levels reflect average blood glucose over approximately what time period, and why?

choices.A: 2–3 months, corresponding to the average lifespan of red blood cells during which hemoglobin is non-enzymatically glycated
choices.B: 1–2 weeks, corresponding to the half-life of serum albumin
choices.C: 24 hours, because glycated hemoglobin is cleared daily by the spleen
choices.D: 6–12 months, corresponding to the lifespan of bone marrow stem cells

explanations.A: Correct. Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) is formed by the non-enzymatic glycation of hemoglobin — glucose molecules covalently attach to the N-terminal valine of the β-globin chain through a Schiff base intermediate that undergoes Amadori rearrangement to form a stable ketoamine. Because this reaction is irreversible and proportional to ambient glucose concentration, HbA1c accumulates over the ~120-day lifespan of red blood cells. The measured HbA1c at any time point reflects a weighted average of blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months, with more recent weeks contributing disproportionately. This makes HbA1c an invaluable clinical marker for long-term glycemic control.
explanations.B: Serum albumin glycation (fructosamine) does reflect glucose levels over 2–3 weeks due to albumin's ~20-day half-life, but this is a different assay from HbA1c. The HbA1c test specifically measures glycated hemoglobin, which persists for the entire red blood cell lifespan of ~120 days, providing a much longer temporal window.
explanations.C: Glycated hemoglobin is not cleared daily. Once hemoglobin is glycated, the modification is permanent for the life of that red blood cell. The spleen removes aged and damaged red blood cells after approximately 120 days, but it does not selectively target glycated hemoglobin on a daily basis.
explanations.D: Bone marrow stem cells are self-renewing and have lifespans measured in years to decades, not the 2–3 month window reflected by HbA1c. The relevant lifespan is that of mature circulating red blood cells (~120 days), not their precursors. Red blood cells lose their nuclei during maturation and cannot synthesize new hemoglobin, so glycation accumulated during their circulation is preserved.

questions.stem: At 120 minutes, Group C glucose remains at 228 mg/dL while insulin is 55.2 μU/mL. Compared to Group A (glucose 92, insulin 18.4), what does this indicate about Group C's β-cell function?

choices.A: Group C β-cells are producing adequate insulin but target tissues are completely unresponsive
choices.B: Group C β-cells have been completely destroyed by autoimmune attack
choices.C: Group C β-cells are secreting insulin but the response is inadequate relative to the degree of hyperglycemia, reflecting partial β-cell failure superimposed on insulin resistance
choices.D: Group C insulin levels are normal, indicating no metabolic abnormality

explanations.A: Complete unresponsiveness to insulin would constitute extreme insulin resistance. While Group C has significant insulin resistance, the fact that insulin levels are elevated (55.2 vs. 18.4 μU/mL) but glucose remains very high (228 vs. 92 mg/dL) suggests the insulin response is not just meeting resistance but is also quantitatively insufficient for the glucose load. If β-cell function were fully intact, insulin levels would be even higher in an attempt to overcome the resistance.
explanations.B: Complete β-cell destruction (as in Type 1 diabetes) would result in undetectable or very low insulin levels. Group C maintains substantial insulin secretion (55.2 μU/mL at 120 minutes), ruling out autoimmune β-cell destruction. T2DM involves progressive β-cell dysfunction and eventual decline, not acute autoimmune destruction.
explanations.C: Correct. Group C's insulin at 120 minutes (55.2 μU/mL) is roughly 3-fold higher than controls but glucose is 2.5-fold higher (228 vs. 92 mg/dL), indicating that insulin secretion, while elevated, is insufficient to normalize glucose. This reflects the dual pathology of T2DM: peripheral insulin resistance requiring more insulin to achieve the same glucose-lowering effect, combined with relative β-cell insufficiency where the β-cells cannot increase secretion enough to fully compensate. The inadequate insulin response relative to ambient glucose — the insulin secretion-glucose gap — is the hallmark of the transition from IGT to frank T2DM.
explanations.D: Group C's insulin levels are approximately 3 times higher than Group A's at 120 minutes, which is clearly abnormal. Moreover, despite this elevated insulin, glucose remains severely elevated (228 mg/dL, well above the diabetic threshold of 200 mg/dL). Characterizing this as 'no metabolic abnormality' ignores both the inappropriately high insulin and the persistent hyperglycemia.

questions.stem: Insulin binds to a receptor tyrosine kinase on target cells. Which intracellular event occurs immediately after insulin binding?

choices.A: Activation of adenylyl cyclase and production of cyclic AMP
choices.B: Opening of ligand-gated ion channels on the cell surface
choices.C: Autophosphorylation of the receptor β-subunit tyrosine residues, initiating the PI3K/Akt signaling cascade
choices.D: Direct binding of insulin to nuclear DNA to activate gene transcription

explanations.A: Adenylyl cyclase and cAMP are components of G-protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) signaling, not receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) signaling. Hormones such as glucagon and epinephrine (via β-adrenergic receptors) activate adenylyl cyclase. Insulin signals through an entirely different mechanism involving tyrosine phosphorylation cascades.
explanations.B: Ligand-gated ion channels are activated by neurotransmitters (e.g., nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, GABA_A receptors) and mediate rapid ionic signaling at synapses. Insulin signaling operates through enzymatic phosphorylation cascades, which are slower but allow for extensive signal amplification and diverse downstream effects including metabolic regulation, growth, and gene expression.
explanations.C: Correct. The insulin receptor is a heterotetrameric receptor tyrosine kinase consisting of two extracellular α-subunits and two transmembrane β-subunits. When insulin binds to the α-subunits, a conformational change activates the intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity of the β-subunits, which undergo trans-autophosphorylation on specific tyrosine residues. These phosphotyrosines serve as docking sites for insulin receptor substrates (IRS-1/2), which are then phosphorylated and recruit PI3K. PI3K generates PIP3, which activates Akt (protein kinase B), leading to GLUT4 vesicle translocation to the plasma membrane, glycogen synthase activation, and metabolic gene regulation.
explanations.D: Insulin is a peptide hormone that cannot cross the plasma membrane and does not bind DNA directly. Its effects on gene transcription are mediated indirectly through intracellular signaling cascades — particularly the MAPK/ERK pathway for growth-related genes and FOXO transcription factor regulation via Akt. Steroid hormones (cortisol, estrogen, testosterone) are the class that directly binds intracellular/nuclear receptors.

questions.passage:
An endocrinology research group investigated dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid (HPT) axis in 60 patients presenting with suspected thyroid disorders. Serum levels of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), free thyroxine (fT4), and free triiodothyronine (fT3) were measured alongside clinical symptoms.

Table 2. Thyroid Function Tests
| Patient Group | n | TSH (mIU/L) | fT4 (ng/dL) | fT3 (pg/mL) | Primary Symptom |
|--------------|---|-------------|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| Normal controls | 15 | 2.1 ± 0.8 | 1.2 ± 0.2 | 3.1 ± 0.4 | None |
| Group X | 15 | 0.05 ± 0.02 | 3.8 ± 0.6 | 8.2 ± 1.1 | Weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance |
| Group Y | 15 | 45.2 ± 12.1 | 0.3 ± 0.1 | 1.1 ± 0.3 | Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain |
| Group Z | 15 | 8.4 ± 2.8 | 0.4 ± 0.1 | 1.3 ± 0.3 | Fatigue, dry skin, mild cognitive slowing |

The researchers administered synthetic TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) intravenously to all groups and measured TSH response at 30 minutes.

Table 3. TRH Stimulation Test
| Group | Baseline TSH (mIU/L) | Peak TSH at 30 min (mIU/L) | Fold-Change |
|-------|---------------------|---------------------------|------------|
| Normal | 2.1 | 14.8 | 7.1 |
| Group X | 0.05 | 0.08 | 1.6 |
| Group Y | 45.2 | 198.4 | 4.4 |
| Group Z | 8.4 | 11.2 | 1.3 |

The group noted that Group Z's blunted TRH response (fold-change 1.3 vs. 7.1 in normals) suggested pituitary-level dysfunction rather than primary thyroid failure.
questions.stem: Group X shows suppressed TSH with elevated fT4 and fT3. This hormonal profile is most consistent with which diagnosis?

choices.A: Primary hypothyroidism with compensatory TSH elevation
choices.B: Iodine deficiency causing decreased thyroid hormone synthesis
choices.D: Primary hyperthyroidism, such as Graves' disease, with negative feedback suppression of TSH

explanations.A: Primary hypothyroidism would show elevated TSH (due to loss of negative feedback) with low fT4 and fT3, which matches Group Y's profile, not Group X's. Group X has the opposite pattern: suppressed TSH with elevated thyroid hormones. Primary hypothyroidism cannot produce elevated fT4 and fT3.
explanations.B: Iodine deficiency reduces thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to low fT4 and compensatory TSH elevation (similar to Group Y). Group X has elevated thyroid hormones, which is inconsistent with iodine deficiency. Iodine is required as a substrate for T4 and T3 synthesis; its absence cannot produce hormone excess.
explanations.C: A TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma would show elevated TSH alongside elevated fT4 and fT3 (secondary hyperthyroidism). Group X has profoundly suppressed TSH (0.05 mIU/L), ruling out a TSH-producing tumor. Pituitary adenomas causing hyperthyroidism are rare and present with the opposite TSH pattern.
explanations.D: Correct. Group X's profile — suppressed TSH (0.05 mIU/L), elevated fT4 (3.8 ng/dL), and elevated fT3 (8.2 pg/mL) with symptoms of weight loss, tremor, and heat intolerance — is the classic pattern of primary hyperthyroidism. The thyroid gland autonomously overproduces T4 and T3 (as in Graves' disease, where TSH receptor-stimulating antibodies drive hormone synthesis). The elevated thyroid hormones exert negative feedback on the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary, suppressing TRH and TSH secretion. The blunted TRH stimulation response (fold-change 1.6) confirms that the pituitary thyrotrophs are maximally suppressed by high circulating T4/T3.

questions.stem: Group Y's markedly elevated absolute TSH peak after TRH stimulation (reaching 198.4 mIU/L from a baseline of 45.2 mIU/L) is best explained by which mechanism?

choices.A: Their pituitary thyrotrophs have upregulated TRH receptors due to chronic absence of thyroid hormone negative feedback
choices.B: The TRH administered was a higher dose than given to other groups
choices.C: Group Y patients have thyroid hormone receptor mutations making them insensitive to T4
choices.D: The high baseline TSH saturated pituitary receptors, preventing any further response

explanations.A: Correct. In primary hypothyroidism (Group Y), the thyroid gland fails to produce adequate T4 and T3. The chronically low thyroid hormone levels remove the normal negative feedback on anterior pituitary thyrotrophs. Without T4/T3-mediated suppression, thyrotrophs upregulate TRH receptor expression and increase their sensitivity to TRH stimulation. This 'priming' effect means that when exogenous TRH is administered, the thyrotrophs respond with an amplified TSH release (198.4 mIU/L from a baseline of 45.2). The exaggerated response confirms intact pituitary function and places the defect at the thyroid level — the defining feature of primary versus secondary hypothyroidism.
explanations.B: The passage states that synthetic TRH was administered to 'all groups,' implying a standardized protocol. The same TRH dose given to normals produced a 7.1-fold increase, while Group Y showed a 4.4-fold increase from a much higher baseline. The absolute peak (198.4 mIU/L) reflects the amplified sensitivity of chronically understimulated thyrotrophs, not a dosing difference.
explanations.C: Thyroid hormone receptor resistance (resistance to thyroid hormone syndrome, RTH) would present with elevated T4/T3 and non-suppressed TSH, quite different from Group Y's profile of low thyroid hormones and high TSH. RTH patients are clinically euthyroid or mildly symptomatic despite high circulating hormone levels.
explanations.D: Receptor saturation would blunt the response, not amplify it. If pituitary TRH receptors were saturated, additional TRH would produce minimal further TSH secretion. Group Y shows the opposite: a robust additional TSH release of >150 mIU/L above baseline, indicating substantial reserve secretory capacity and heightened receptor sensitivity.

questions.stem: Group Z has moderately elevated TSH, low fT4, and a blunted TRH response. This combination is most consistent with which level of HPT axis dysfunction?

choices.A: Primary thyroid failure (thyroid gland itself is defective)
choices.B: Secondary hypothyroidism due to pituitary dysfunction, with the mildly elevated TSH representing biologically inactive TSH isoforms
choices.C: Tertiary hypothyroidism due to hypothalamic TRH deficiency
choices.D: Peripheral thyroid hormone resistance at the tissue level

explanations.A: Primary thyroid failure would produce an exaggerated TRH response (as seen in Group Y), because the intact pituitary would be sensitized by chronic low T4. Group Z's blunted TRH response (fold-change 1.3 vs. 7.1 in normals) directly contradicts primary thyroid pathology — it indicates the pituitary itself cannot appropriately increase TSH secretion in response to stimulation.
explanations.B: Correct. Group Z's blunted TRH response despite low thyroid hormones points to pituitary-level dysfunction (secondary hypothyroidism). The moderately elevated baseline TSH (8.4 mIU/L) might seem to contradict pituitary failure, but in secondary hypothyroidism, the pituitary can produce immunoreactive TSH that is detected by the assay but is biologically inactive due to abnormal glycosylation patterns. These TSH isoforms have reduced ability to stimulate the TSH receptor on thyroid follicular cells, resulting in low T4 output despite measurable TSH. The blunted TRH stimulation response confirms limited pituitary reserve, distinguishing this from primary thyroid failure.
explanations.C: Tertiary hypothyroidism (TRH deficiency) would typically show low baseline TSH that increases substantially with exogenous TRH, because the pituitary thyrotrophs are intact but understimulated. Group Z's blunted TRH response (poor pituitary response) argues against a purely hypothalamic defect and suggests intrinsic pituitary dysfunction.
explanations.D: Peripheral thyroid hormone resistance would present with elevated T4/T3 and non-suppressed TSH (the receptors cannot detect the hormone, so feedback is impaired). Group Z shows low fT4 and fT3, which is inconsistent with resistance where hormone production is typically normal or elevated to compensate.

questions.stem: Thyroxine (T4) is converted to the more active triiodothyronine (T3) in peripheral tissues. Which enzyme catalyzes this conversion?

explanations.A: Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) catalyzes the oxidation and organification of iodide and the coupling of iodotyrosines (MIT and DIT) within the thyroid gland to form T4 and T3. However, TPO acts within the thyroid, not in peripheral tissues. The peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 requires removal of a specific iodine atom, which TPO does not perform.
explanations.B: Correct. Type I 5'-deiodinase (predominantly in liver and kidney) and type II 5'-deiodinase (in brain, pituitary, brown adipose tissue, and thyroid) catalyze the outer-ring deiodination of T4, removing an iodine atom from the 5' position of the phenolic ring to produce T3. Approximately 80% of circulating T3 is generated by this peripheral conversion rather than direct thyroid secretion. Type III deiodinase performs inner-ring deiodination to produce the inactive metabolite reverse T3 (rT3). These deiodinases are selenocysteine-containing enzymes, making selenium an essential trace element for thyroid hormone metabolism.
explanations.C: Tyrosine hydroxylase catalyzes the rate-limiting step of catecholamine biosynthesis, converting L-tyrosine to L-DOPA in the adrenal medulla and catecholaminergic neurons. It has no role in thyroid hormone metabolism. Despite the shared tyrosine precursor, the pathways for catecholamines and thyroid hormones are entirely distinct.
explanations.D: Monoamine oxidase (MAO) degrades monoamine neurotransmitters (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine) by oxidative deamination. It plays no role in thyroid hormone activation. Thyroid hormones are iodinated tyrosine derivatives metabolized by deiodinases and conjugation enzymes, not by MAO.

questions.stem: If a patient in Group X were treated with a thionamide drug (such as methimazole) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, what changes would be expected in their TSH and fT4 levels after several weeks?

choices.A: TSH would increase toward normal as fT4 decreases, due to partial release from negative feedback suppression
choices.B: TSH would decrease further while fT4 remains elevated
choices.C: Both TSH and fT4 would increase simultaneously
choices.D: There would be no change because thionamides do not affect thyroid hormone levels

explanations.A: Correct. Methimazole inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO), which is essential for iodide oxidation, organification, and the coupling of MIT and DIT to form T4 and T3 within thyroglobulin. By blocking new hormone synthesis, fT4 and fT3 levels gradually decline over weeks as existing hormone stores are depleted and circulating hormones are metabolized. As fT4 falls, the negative feedback on anterior pituitary thyrotrophs weakens, allowing TSH secretion to increase toward the normal range. The time course of weeks reflects the large intrathyroidal hormone reserve and the ~7-day half-life of T4 in circulation.
explanations.B: Methimazole inhibits thyroid peroxidase, reducing the synthesis of T4 and T3. As fT4 levels fall, negative feedback suppression of the pituitary is relieved, causing TSH to rise. A further decrease in TSH would require even greater thyroid hormone-mediated suppression, which is the opposite of what thionamide treatment produces.
explanations.C: Simultaneous increases in both TSH and fT4 would only occur in TSH-secreting pituitary adenomas or thyroid hormone resistance — conditions where the normal feedback relationship is disrupted. In pharmacological treatment of hyperthyroidism, the expected response is declining fT4 (drug effect) and rising TSH (feedback release), not parallel increases.
explanations.D: Thionamides are established first-line treatments for hyperthyroidism precisely because they effectively reduce thyroid hormone synthesis. Methimazole and propylthiouracil have well-documented mechanisms of action through TPO inhibition (and PTU additionally blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion). Clinical trials consistently demonstrate their efficacy in normalizing thyroid function.
